competitors.label: Angular Services Tutorial
competitors.selectionReason: This is the clearest official service-introduction page and the strongest canonical baseline for why Angular teams use services.
theirStrengths: It clearly explains that services separate shared data and logic from components and shows how DI wires them in.
theirGaps: It is more introductory and lighter on provider scope, root versus local lifetime, and architectural boundary decisions.

followUpConfusion: The value is not the class itself — it’s the lifetime + sharing + testability you get from DI and a clean separation from UI code.
Service lifetime is determined by where it’s provided . Angular DI is hierarchical: child injectors can override parent providers.
realWorldUseCases: When root scope is the wrong choice
actionableExamples: Each CheckoutWizardComponent gets its own WizardStateService

realWorldUseCases: The tutorial already grounds services in shared data access and reuse across components, so the practical-use comparison stays close.
actionableExamples: It includes a concrete service extraction walkthrough, so the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: It is lighter on provider-scope trade-offs and on why lifetime changes the behavior of a service in production code.

realWorldUseCases: Add one example where root scope is wrong because a service should be isolated per component instance.
actionableExamples: Add a compact root-provided versus component-provided code pair.
followUpConfusion: Keep one compact note on keeping purely presentational transforms out of services.

competitors.winReason: Our page now pairs DI scope with a concrete root-versus-component provider example, which makes the service-lifetime and ownership explanation more actionable than the tutorial.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Angular Basic Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd’s Angular basics guide includes a dedicated services section, making it the strongest interview-prep comparator.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd explains what a service is, but our page is more explicit about lifetime, sharing, testability, and how provider scope changes behavior.
theirStrengths: It gives a clean definition of services, common uses, and the providedIn root follow-up.
theirGaps: It is lighter on scope guardrails and on the deeper architectural reason teams move logic into services.

realWorldUseCases: The value is not the class itself — it’s the lifetime + sharing + testability you get from DI and a clean separation from UI code.
followUpConfusion: Service lifetime is determined by where it’s provided . Angular DI is hierarchical: child injectors can override parent providers.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd explains shared business logic and data operations, but not as strongly through lifetime and architectural boundaries.
actionableExamples: Its service examples are concrete enough that the example comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: The guide leaves more of the provider-scope and service-lifetime follow-up to the reader.

actionableExamples: Add one example showing a root singleton and a component-scoped wizard state service side by side.

competitors.label: Stack Overflow: Why do we need services when we have components?
competitors.selectionReason: This community thread is a direct comparator because it addresses the practical reason Angular teams split work into services.
competitors.winReason: The Stack Overflow answers justify reuse and sharing, but our page is stronger on lifetime, scope, and architectural boundary design.
theirStrengths: It gives an intuitive reuse-and-sharing explanation that many developers recognize immediately.
theirGaps: It does not go as far into scope, override behavior, or how services keep UI code predictable over time.

realWorldUseCases: The value is not the class itself — it’s the lifetime + sharing + testability you get from DI and a clean separation from UI code.
followUpConfusion: Service lifetime is determined by where it’s provided . Angular DI is hierarchical: child injectors can override parent providers.

realWorldUseCases: The thread explains that services are reusable and can be shared more broadly than components, but it stays at a generic level.
actionableExamples: The examples and explanations are practical enough that the example comparison remains close.
followUpConfusion: It is much lighter on provider scope and on the root-versus-local instance follow-up that interviewers often ask.

actionableExamples: Add a tiny example where providing a service at the component level intentionally isolates state between two copies of the same component.
